class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Supply Chain Management and Strategy
learning_objectives: Define supply chain management and its strategic importance
Map the end-to-end supply chain from raw materials to end consumer
Explain how supply chain strategy must align with business strategy
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management Ch. 1-2
Fisher, What Is the Right Supply Chain for Your Product? (HBR)

lecture_notes.introduction: Supply chain management coordinates the flow of materials, information, and finances across all parties involved in delivering a product to the end customer. It is a source of competitive advantage.
key_concepts: Supply chain as a network: suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, retailers, customers
Supply chain drivers: facilities, inventory, transportation, information, sourcing, pricing
Efficient vs. responsive supply chains (Fisher's framework)
Supply chain strategy alignment with product characteristics
Total cost of ownership vs. unit cost thinking
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping Zara's supply chain and explaining how its responsive design enables 2-week fashion cycles vs. industry average of 6 months
setup_requirements: Textbook: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.)
Supply chain mapping software or templates

review.value: Minimizes cost for predictable, low-margin products; emphasizes economies of scale and asset utilization
Prioritizes speed and flexibility for innovative, uncertain-demand products; accepts higher cost for faster response

class_schedule.topic: Demand Forecasting and Planning in Supply Chains
learning_objectives: Calculate forecast accuracy and bias metrics
Explain the bullwhip effect and its causes
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management Ch. 7
Lee et al., The Bullwhip Effect (MIT Sloan)

lecture_notes.introduction: Accurate demand signals are the foundation of supply chain planning. Poor forecasting leads to either excess inventory (waste) or stockouts (lost sales and customer dissatisfaction).
key_concepts: Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment (CPFR)
Demand sensing vs. demand shaping
Bullwhip effect: causes and countermeasures
S&OP (Sales and Operations Planning) process
Forecast accuracy metrics: MAPE, bias, tracking signal
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulating the bullwhip effect using the beer distribution game, then discussing countermeasures

review.value: Demand variability amplifies as you move upstream in the supply chain; caused by demand signal processing, order batching, price fluctuations, and rationing
Cross-functional process aligning demand plans with supply plans to balance customer service with cost and inventory targets

learning_objectives: Optimize inventory positioning across the supply chain
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management Ch. 8, 11

lecture_notes.introduction: Where you hold inventory and how you configure your distribution network determines your ability to deliver to customers quickly and cost-effectively.
key_concepts: Inventory types: cycle stock, safety stock, pipeline, seasonal
Safety stock = z x sigma x sqrt(lead time)
Distribution network design options: direct, via DC, cross-dock
Network optimization: facility location, number of DCs, allocation
Risk pooling: centralized inventory reduces total safety stock
lecture_notes.demonstration: Network design exercise: comparing total cost and service level of 1 DC vs. 4 regional DCs for a national distributor

review.value: Aggregating demand across locations reduces variability and allows lower total safety stock while maintaining service levels
More facilities = faster delivery and lower transport cost to customer, but higher facility cost and more total inventory

learning_objectives: Develop sourcing strategies for different supply categories
Evaluate suppliers using total cost of ownership
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management Ch. 13
Kraljic, Purchasing Must Become Supply Management (HBR)

lecture_notes.introduction: Strategic sourcing goes beyond buying at the lowest price. It involves understanding supply markets, managing supplier relationships, and mitigating supply risk.
key_concepts: Kraljic matrix: strategic, leverage, bottleneck, non-critical items
Single vs. dual vs. multi-sourcing strategies
Total cost of ownership: purchase price + quality + delivery + service + risk
Supplier evaluation: quality, cost, delivery, flexibility, innovation
Supplier development and collaborative partnerships
lecture_notes.demonstration: Kraljic matrix exercise: categorizing a company's purchases and developing appropriate sourcing strategies for each quadrant

review.value: Classifies purchases by supply risk (y-axis) and profit impact (x-axis) into four quadrants requiring different sourcing strategies
Full lifecycle cost including purchase price, transportation, quality costs, inventory holding, and risk of disruption

learning_objectives: Compare transportation modes and their cost-service tradeoffs
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management Ch. 14

lecture_notes.introduction: Transportation is typically the largest single cost in the supply chain. Optimizing transportation decisions balances cost with speed and reliability.
key_concepts: Transportation modes: truck, rail, air, ocean, intermodal
Cost-service tradeoff: faster modes cost more per unit
Vehicle routing problem and optimization
Cross-docking and milk-run strategies
3PL vs. 4PL: outsourcing logistics operations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Route optimization exercise: designing delivery routes for a regional distributor using optimization software to minimize total distance and cost
setup_requirements: Route optimization tool or Excel solver

review.value: Truck: flexible and fast for short-medium distances. Rail: low cost for bulk. Air: fastest but expensive. Ocean: lowest cost for international bulk
Products move through a DC without being put into storage; reduces inventory holding and handling costs while maintaining delivery speed

class_schedule.topic: Supply Chain Risk, Resilience, and Sustainability
learning_objectives: Identify and assess supply chain risk categories
Design resilient supply chains with appropriate buffers
Integrate sustainability into supply chain strategy
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management Ch. 6

lecture_notes.introduction: COVID-19, the Suez Canal blockage, and semiconductor shortages demonstrated that supply chain disruptions can cripple entire economies. Resilience and sustainability are now strategic imperatives.
key_concepts: Supply chain risk categories: supply, demand, operational, environmental, geopolitical
Resilience strategies: redundancy, flexibility, visibility, collaboration
Supply chain mapping for risk identification
Sustainability: carbon footprint, circular economy, responsible sourcing
ESG reporting and supply chain transparency requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Supply chain risk assessment for a consumer electronics company: mapping critical dependencies and designing resilience strategies

review.value: Resilience requires buffers (inventory, capacity, suppliers) that reduce efficiency; the optimal balance depends on risk tolerance and disruption probability
Designing products and supply chains for reuse, repair, refurbishment, and recycling rather than linear take-make-dispose

quizzes.topic: Supply Chain Strategy and Demand Planning

questions.question: Explain the bullwhip effect using a specific example, and describe two strategies a manufacturer could implement to reduce it.
questions.answer: Example: A 10% increase in consumer demand at a retailer leads to a 20% order increase to the distributor, which orders 40% more from the manufacturer, causing massive overcapacity and subsequent inventory glut. Strategies: (1) Share point-of-sale data directly with upstream partners (information sharing), eliminating the need for each level to forecast independently. (2) Implement everyday low pricing instead of promotions that cause demand spikes and forward buying. Additional strategies include reducing order batching through smaller, more frequent orders and vendor-managed inventory.

rubric.key: Clear bullwhip effect example with amplification
Two specific and effective reduction strategies

questions.question: Using Fisher's framework, would you recommend an efficient or responsive supply chain for a fashion retailer selling trendy clothing? Justify with at least three product characteristics.
questions.answer: Responsive supply chain. Product characteristics: (1) High demand uncertainty: trendy fashion has unpredictable demand with short selling windows, making accurate forecasting difficult. (2) Short product life cycle: trends change seasonally or faster, so unsold inventory becomes worthless. (3) High product variety: many styles, colors, and sizes create SKU proliferation. (4) High margin: fashion markups support the higher cost of responsive operations. An efficient supply chain optimized for cost would result in either massive markdowns on unsold inventory or stockouts on trending items.

rubric.key: Explains consequences of wrong supply chain type

questions.question: A company sources a critical component from a single supplier in East Asia. Using the Kraljic matrix, classify this item and recommend a sourcing strategy change.
questions.answer: This is a Strategic item (high profit impact because it is critical, high supply risk because single source in distant geography). Current single-sourcing strategy is risky. Recommended changes: (1) Develop a secondary qualified supplier, ideally in a different geographic region, to reduce concentration risk. (2) Maintain strategic partnership with primary supplier while building the alternative. (3) Increase safety stock buffer for this component to bridge any transition period. (4) Invest in supplier development to improve primary supplier's resilience. The goal is to maintain the benefits of a deep relationship while reducing catastrophic single-point-of-failure risk.

questions.question: A company ships 500 pallets per week from a single warehouse to 50 retail locations across the country. Compare hub-and-spoke vs. direct shipping and recommend an approach.
questions.answer: Direct shipping: Each location receives individual shipments from the warehouse. Advantage: faster transit, simpler routing. Disadvantage: many small shipments = high per-unit transport cost and low truck utilization. Hub-and-spoke: Ship full truckloads to regional hubs, then distribute locally. Advantage: consolidated trunk line moves reduce cost per pallet; allows cross-docking. Disadvantage: adds handling and transit time. Recommendation: Hub-and-spoke with 3-4 regional cross-dock facilities. At 500 pallets/week to 50 locations (average 10 pallets per location per week), individual direct shipments would be too small for efficient truckload. Hub-and-spoke consolidation can reduce transport cost by 15-25% while adding only 1 day of transit time.

major_assignments.title: Supply Chain Strategy and Optimization Project
major_assignments.description: Analyze a real company's supply chain, identify optimization opportunities, and develop a comprehensive supply chain improvement plan including network design, inventory strategy, sourcing recommendations, and risk mitigation.
objectives: Map and analyze a real company's end-to-end supply chain
Identify specific improvement opportunities using SCM frameworks
Quantify potential savings and service improvements
Present a professional supply chain improvement plan

criteria: Accurate supply chain map with all key nodes
Thorough current-state analysis with accurate supply chain mapping
Quantified improvement recommendations using SCM frameworks
Comprehensive risk assessment with mitigation strategies

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of SCM concepts and quantitative techniques

case_discussions.description: Active participation in supply chain case study discussions

major_assignments.description: Supply chain strategy and optimization project

midterm_examination.description: Covers supply chain strategy, demand planning, inventory, and sourcing

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including transportation, logistics, risk, and sustainability

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance required. Supply chain simulation exercises cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Project milestones penalized 10% per day. Beer game and simulation participation is mandatory.
course_policies.collaboration: Supply chain project may be completed in teams of 2-3. Quizzes and exams are individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Company analysis must use publicly available data and proper attribution. Fabricating supply chain data is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Excel with Solver add-in. Supply chain simulation software. Access to industry databases.
